name: Lobster
summary: Wouter van Oortmerssen's statically-typed Python-ish language using flow-sensitive lifetime analysis to elide ~95% of RC ops at compile time, with a custom bump-style allocator for the rest. Lifetime analysis plays a borrow-checker-like role; cycles banned in practice, leak-on-exit reports as a pragmatic escape.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Lobster is a statically-typed Python-ish language using flow-sensitive lifetime analysis to elide ~95% of RC ops at compile time, with a custom bump-style allocator for the rest. Its lifetime analysis plays a borrow-checker-like role: it tracks ownership through flow-sensitive type inference and specialisation, removing inc/dec on locally-scoped references entirely.

Inline structs (zero-overhead values) plus race-free, GIL-free multithreading via a distributed memory model mean RC objects do not cross thread boundaries — sidestepping the atomic-RC cost that haunts Swift and CPython. Cost is claimed within an order of magnitude of C, far above Python/Lua.

Status (as of 2026-04): production single-author language with active development; not widely deployed but a clean reference for what flow-sensitive RC elision plus a small custom runtime can achieve in a Python-shaped surface syntax.

sections.title: Flow-sensitive lifetime analysis
sections.content:
Lobster's lifetime analysis is borrow-checker-adjacent: it tracks where references flow, which scopes own them, and which uses are read-only borrows. When a reference's lifetime is provably contained within its enclosing scope, the compiler emits no `inc`/`dec` at all — the value is known to outlive every use, and the destructor at scope exit covers cleanup. Specialisation extends this: polymorphic functions are monomorphised on the lifetime of their argument so the elision survives across call boundaries.

The author claims ~95% of RC ops are elided. The remaining ~5% — mostly references that escape into heap-resident data structures or get returned from functions in non-tail position — go through a custom bump-style allocator with explicit ref-count fields, lighter than a general malloc-style heap.

sections.title: Cycles, threading, leak-at-exit
sections.content:
Cycles are banned in practice. If leaked, they are reported at exit with a human-readable diagnostic — a clever pragmatic move: the cycle problem is pushed back to the programmer, but with tooling support rather than silent leaks. Compared to Swift (programmer manages `weak`/`unowned`), Nim ORC (Bacon trial-deletion at runtime), and Lean 4 (cycles banned by language), Lobster occupies a fourth point: cycles allowed, leaked, reported.

The race-free, GIL-free multithreading model uses a distributed memory layout where each thread owns a private heap and inter-thread communication goes through explicit message passing. RC objects do not cross thread boundaries, which means RC ops can stay non-atomic in the common path — the same simplification that makes Lean 4's positive-RC fast path work, achieved by language convention rather than a sign-bit sentinel.
